exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.title: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: In spite of there (1) ...... a great deal of interest in sustainable design, there (2) ...... to be a considerable gap between what studios promise and what they actually deliver.
items.explanation: Despite + there → **being** interest; there **seems** to be a gap.

items.sentence: There are plenty (3) ...... examples of designers who have insisted (4) ...... expressing their creativity freely and have faced professional setbacks for it.

If you are going to produce controversial work, you've got to be prepared (5) ...... the reaction it may provoke. That reaction may not always be positive. You may be stared (6) ......, criticised (7) ...... expressing yourself so unconventionally, or even ridiculed.
items.explanation: Prepared **for** the reaction; stared **at**; criticised **for** expressing yourself.

items.sentence: On the other hand, you may be complimented (8) ...... your originality and vision. Context is very important in the art world. At a commercial gallery, for (9) ......, you should probably be more anxious (10) ...... appeal to buyers than focused purely on artistic expression.
items.explanation: Complimented **on** originality; for **example**; anxious **to** appeal to buyers.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.title: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: The director hired a professional (STYLE) ...... to create a coherent visual identity for the entire exhibition.
items.explanation: Person who styles → professional **stylist** (noun from STYLE).

items.sentence: There is a remarkable (SIMILAR) ...... between the early works of these two celebrated artists.
You should lower your (EXPECT) ...... a little; not every piece sells the first time it is shown.
items.explanation: Plural noun from EXPECT → lower your **expectations**.

items.sentence: Let me know when you reach a (DECIDE) ...... about which art school you plan to apply to.
Critics have greeted the new installation with great (ENTHUSE) .......
items.explanation: Noun from ENTHUSE → greeted with great **enthusiasm**.

items.sentence: The studio has increased its (PRODUCE) ...... of handmade ceramic pieces to keep up with rising demand.
That sculpture is absolutely (BEAUTY) ......!
These ceramic pieces are designed to appeal to serious collectors and casual buyers (LIKE) .......

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
You really should get yourself a new camera. **about**
It ...... a new camera.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is long overdue, so the correct answer is "is about time you got".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is about time you got", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I hate it when you wear that ridiculous oversized beret! **wish**
I ...... wear that ridiculous oversized beret!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present wish, so the correct answer is "wish you would not".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "wish you would not", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Natalie regrets wearing such formal clothes to the studio open day. **had**
Natalie ...... such formal clothes to the studio open day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past regret, so the correct answer is "wishes she had not worn".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "wishes she had not worn", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I bought the vintage jacket even though it was second-hand. **despite**
I bought the vintage jacket ...... second-hand.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is despite + gerund, so the correct answer is "despite it being".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "despite it being", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
It's a real shame I can't get to the exhibition this weekend! **only**
If ...... get to the exhibition this weekend!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wistful if only, so the correct answer is "only I could".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "only I could", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
In spite of my pleading with her all evening, Sophie refused to lend me her new scarf. **though**
Sophie refused to lend me her new scarf ...... all evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is concession, so the correct answer is "even though I begged her".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "even though I begged her", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I don't want you to adopt exactly the same creative style as mine. **rather**
I ...... adopt exactly the same creative style as mine.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is preference about another person, so the correct answer is "would rather you did not".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "would rather you did not", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I can't go to the gallery opening as I have nothing suitable to wear. **something**
I'd go to the gallery opening ...... suitable to wear!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is second conditional, so the correct answer is "if I had something".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "if I had something", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

items.sentence:
Despite being a retiree, Elena still pushes herself to create bold new work. **fact**
In spite ...... is a retiree, Elena still pushes herself to create bold new work.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is despite the fact, so the correct answer is "of the fact that she".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "of the fact that she", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: I'm just going to pop **C** into that new art supply shop for a couple of minutes.
items.answer: C – into that new art supply shop for a couple of minutes.
acceptedAnswers: C – into that new art supply shop for a couple of minutes.
items.explanation: Pop into → pop **into** that new art supply shop.

items.sentence: I've grown **F** out of these old brushes, so my tutor is going to order me some professional ones.
items.answer: F – out of these old brushes, so my tutor is going to order me some professional ones.
acceptedAnswers: F – out of these old brushes, so my tutor is going to order me some professional ones.
items.explanation: No longer interested → grown **out of** these old brushes.

items.sentence: I'd like to try **A** on that artist's smock you've got hanging near the studio door.
items.answer: A – on that artist's smock you've got hanging near the studio door.
acceptedAnswers: A – on that artist's smock you've got hanging near the studio door.
items.explanation: Try clothing → try **on** that artist's smock.

items.sentence: I never thought that painting on walls would catch **G** on as a legitimate art form, but it has!
items.answer: G – on as a legitimate art form, but it has!
acceptedAnswers: G – on as a legitimate art form, but it has!
items.explanation: Become popular → catch **on** as a legitimate art form.

items.sentence: We all lined **D** up and the photographer took our individual portraits one by one.
items.answer: D – up and the photographer took our individual portraits one by one.
acceptedAnswers: D – up and the photographer took our individual portraits one by one.
items.explanation: Form a queue → lined **up** for portraits.

items.sentence: I'd take **B** off my paint-stained apron, but I think it's stuck to my shirt underneath!
items.answer: B – off my paint-stained apron, but I think it's stuck to my shirt underneath!
acceptedAnswers: B – off my paint-stained apron, but I think it's stuck to my shirt underneath!
items.explanation: Remove clothing → take **off** my paint-stained apron.

items.sentence: Let's all dress **E** up for the end-of-year art school celebration party!
items.answer: E – up for the end-of-year art school celebration party!
acceptedAnswers: E – up for the end-of-year art school celebration party!
items.explanation: Wear smart clothes → dress **up** for the celebration.
